title: Citizenship · Rights & Responsibilities
description: Voting, the duties and rights of citizens, the Pledge and the Oath, taxes and Selective Service, and the amendments that protect rights.

cards.front: There are four amendments about who can vote. Describe one of them.
cards.back: Citizens eighteen (18) and older can vote (26th); you don't have to pay a poll tax to vote (24th); any citizen can vote — women and men (19th); a male citizen of any race can vote (15th). Any one is accepted.
cards.quizAnswer: Any citizen 18 or older can vote, regardless of race, sex, or wealth.
distractors: Only property owners may cast a vote.
Only men over twenty-one may vote.
Voting requires passing a literacy test.
Only native-born citizens may vote.
States may charge a fee to vote.

cards.front: What is one responsibility that is only for United States citizens?
cards.back: To serve on a jury, or to vote in a federal election. These civic duties are reserved for citizens.
cards.quizAnswer: Serve on a jury, or vote in a federal election.
distractors: Pay federal income taxes each year.
Obey all the laws of the nation.
Register men for the Selective Service.
Respect the rights of other people.
Defend the country in a war.

cards.front: What is one right only for United States citizens?
cards.back: To vote in a federal election, or to run for federal office. Non-citizens do not have these rights.
cards.quizAnswer: Vote in a federal election, or run for federal office.
distractors: Freedom of speech and religion.
The right to a fair, fast trial.
The right to keep and bear arms.
Protection from unreasonable searches.
The right to peaceably assemble.

cards.front: What are two rights of everyone living in the United States?
cards.back: Examples: freedom of expression, freedom of speech, freedom of assembly, freedom to petition the government, freedom of religion, and the right to bear arms. These apply to citizens and non-citizens alike.
cards.quizAnswer: Freedom of speech, religion, assembly, petition, and expression.
distractors: Voting in elections and running for office.
Serving on a jury and holding office.
Receiving a U.S. passport and voting.
Working for the federal government only.
Choosing the President and Vice President.

cards.front: When we say the Pledge of Allegiance, what do we show loyalty to?
cards.back: The United States, and the flag. The Pledge is a promise of loyalty to the nation and its flag.
cards.quizAnswer: The United States and the flag.
distractors: The President and the Congress.
The Constitution and the courts.
The state and its governor.
The military and its leaders.
The founders and their ideals.

cards.front: What is one promise you make when you become a United States citizen?
cards.back: Give up loyalty to other countries; defend the Constitution and laws of the U.S.; obey the laws; serve in the military or do important work for the nation if needed; be loyal to the United States. Any one is accepted.
cards.quizAnswer: Give up loyalty to other countries and defend the Constitution and laws.
distractors: Vote in every federal election held.
Pay an annual citizenship membership fee.
Live in the United States permanently forever.
Learn to speak a second language.
Join a major political party.

cards.front: How old do citizens have to be to vote for President?
cards.back: Eighteen (18) and older. The 26th Amendment set the national voting age at 18.
distractors: Sixteen (16) and older.
Twenty-one (21) and older.
Twenty-five (25) and older.
Nineteen (19) and older.
Thirty (30) and older.

cards.front: What are two ways that Americans can participate in their democracy?
cards.back: Examples: vote; join a political party; help with a campaign; join a civic or community group; give an opinion to an official; call or write to officials; publicly support or oppose an issue; run for office; write to a newspaper. Any two are accepted.
cards.quizAnswer: Vote, join a party, contact officials, run for office, or join a civic group.
distractors: Serve on a jury and pay taxes.
Obey the laws and register to drive.
Carry a passport and salute the flag.
Attend public school and find work.
Defend the Constitution and the flag.

cards.front: When is the last day you can send in federal income tax forms?
cards.back: April 15. Federal income tax returns are generally due by April 15 each year.
distractors: January 1.
July 4.
December 31.
March 15.
October 15.

cards.front: When must all men register for the Selective Service?
cards.back: At age eighteen (18); men must register between ages 18 and 26. Registration keeps a list in case a military draft is ever needed.
distractors: At age 16, in high school.
At age 21, when fully adult.
Only when a war begins.
At age 25, before turning 26.
Only if they join the military.

cards.back: It freed the enslaved people and ended (abolished) slavery in the United States. It was ratified in 1865 after the Civil War.
cards.quizAnswer: Ended slavery; freed the enslaved people.
distractors: Gave Black men the right to vote.
Gave women the right to vote.
Granted citizenship and equal protection.
Ended the poll tax in elections.
Lowered the voting age to eighteen.

cards.back: It granted citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S. and guaranteed equal protection of the laws and due process. Ratified in 1868.
cards.quizAnswer: Granted citizenship to all born here and guaranteed equal protection.
distractors: Ended slavery throughout the entire nation.
Gave women the right to vote.
Lowered the national voting age to eighteen.
Removed the poll tax from federal elections.
Gave Congress the power to tax income.

cards.back: It gave (male) citizens the right to vote regardless of race or color. Ratified in 1870, it barred denying the vote based on race.
cards.quizAnswer: Gave citizens the right to vote regardless of race or color.
distractors: Gave women the right to vote.
Lowered the voting age to eighteen.
Ended slavery across the United States.
Removed the poll tax in elections.
Granted citizenship to former slaves.

cards.back: It gave women the right to vote. Ratified in 1920, it barred denying the vote based on sex.
cards.quizAnswer: Gave women the right to vote.
distractors: Gave Black men the right to vote.
Lowered the voting age to eighteen.
Ended the poll tax in elections.
Ended slavery throughout the nation.
Granted citizenship to all persons born here.

cards.back: It ended (banned) the poll tax in federal elections. Ratified in 1964, it stopped states from charging a fee to vote.
cards.quizAnswer: Ended the poll tax in federal elections.
distractors: Gave women the right to vote.
Lowered the voting age to eighteen.
Gave Black men the right to vote.
Ended slavery in the United States.
Set presidential term limits at two.

cards.back: It lowered the voting age to eighteen (18). Ratified in 1971, it set the national minimum voting age at 18.
cards.quizAnswer: Lowered the voting age to eighteen (18).
distractors: Ended the poll tax in elections.
Gave women the right to vote.
Gave citizens of any race the vote.
Set the presidential term at four years.
Granted citizenship to all born here.

cards.back: The right to bear arms — the right to keep and carry weapons. It is part of the Bill of Rights.
cards.quizAnswer: The right to keep and bear arms (to have a gun).
distractors: The right to free speech and press.
The right against unreasonable searches.
The right to a fast and fair trial.
The right to practice any religion.
The right to peaceably assemble.

cards.back: It protects against self-incrimination (you don't have to testify against yourself) and guarantees due process of law; a person cannot be tried twice for the same crime or have property taken without fair payment.
cards.quizAnswer: The right against self-incrimination and to due process of law.
distractors: The right to keep and bear arms.
The right to free speech and religion.
The right to a speedy, public trial.
Protection from unreasonable searches.
The right of citizens to vote.
